---
title: "Master's Thesis"
subtitle: "Beyond Beliefs: structural determinants of agricultural-plastic end-of-life management among California strawberry growers"
date: today
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Program**: M.S. Environmental Science and Management, Cal Poly San Luis Obispo
**Advisor**: Nick Williams
**Status**: Defended July 2026, in post-defense revision
:::

## Question

What predicts how California strawberry growers dispose of used agricultural
plastic mulch: their attitudes, their farm characteristics, or their location?
Plastic mulch is central to strawberry production, and its end-of-life handling,
landfill versus recycling, carries real environmental cost. Most work on grower
behavior starts from beliefs. This thesis asks whether structure, farm and
place, explains more than belief does.

## Approach

The analysis uses a statewide mail survey of California strawberry growers.
Growers answered batteries of Likert-scale statements about plastic mulch,
environmental concern, and where they get their information. The core of the
work is a two-stage design: first recover the attitude types hidden in those
answers, then test whether type, farm, or place predicts what growers actually
do with their used plastic.

## Latent class analysis: finding hidden grower types

Latent class analysis is a way to find groups inside a population without
deciding the groups in advance. Instead of sorting growers into categories I
picked, the method looks at the pattern of answers across many survey questions
and asks whether people fall into a small number of distinct response profiles.
It treats each grower's answers as evidence of an underlying attitude type that
you cannot observe directly, and estimates the probability that each grower
belongs to each type. That fits the goal here. I wanted the grower typologies to
come out of the data, not out of my own assumptions about who growers are. The
models are fit with the poLCA package in R, a standard tool for this kind of
categorical survey data.

## Choosing the number of types

How many groups exist is itself a modeling decision, not something you set ahead
of time. For each battery of questions I fit a series of candidate models,
starting with two groups and adding one at a time. Each candidate gets scored on
how well it fits the data against how complex it is, mainly using the Bayesian
Information Criterion, with related measures checked alongside it so the choice
never rests on a single number. Two things matter beyond the scores. Class
separation asks whether each grower's answers point cleanly to one group, since a
model can score well and still produce fuzzy, overlapping types. Interpretability
asks whether the resulting profiles actually make sense and can be given a clear
label. A model with a slightly better score but muddier groups does not win over
a simpler one with clean separation.

## From types to behavior

Once each grower has a most-likely type, that type becomes a variable in its own
right, used the same way as acreage or years in operation. The second stage tests
whether attitude type, farm characteristics, or growing region are associated
with how growers dispose of their used mulch. Because many variables get tested
against the same outcome, the analysis corrects for multiple comparisons, so a
result has to clear a higher bar before it counts as a real pattern rather than
noise. The payoff of the two-stage design is a sharper question: not just whether
attitudes and behavior seem related in general, but whether a grower's specific,
properly identified type is what drives what they do with their plastic.

## Tools

- **Data**: statewide mail survey of California strawberry growers
- **Analysis**: latent class analysis (poLCA) for the typologies, then tests of
  type, farm, and region against disposal outcomes with multiple-comparison
  correction
- **Pipeline**: R (tidyverse, poLCA, ggplot2), reproducible, with a numbers
  ledger locking each reported statistic to its source script

## Related work

This thesis has been presented as work in progress at the AFHVS/ASFS Annual
Meeting, the IASNR Conference, and the Society for Applied Anthropology Annual
Meeting in 2026.

*The full document will be available after final submission.*

<!-- [VERIFY] Sample size omitted on purpose: vault sources conflict (126 of
1,014 vs 121 of 800). No unpublished numeric results are shown here per the
thesis privacy rule. Add figures only once reconciled and cleared to publish. -->
